quality.rationale: Derives geodesic deviation with external EM force, then uses dimensional scaling to resolve equivalence principle paradox.

origin.question: Problem 12.14. Consider two particles of equal mass in a freely falling elevator. One carries a charge $q$ and the other is neutral. There is a vertical electrical field $E$ in the elevator. Write an equation for the separation of the particles as a function of time, including tidal and electrical effects. Reconcile the existence of both terms with the equivalence principle.
origin.solution: Since the particles start from rest, if we confine our observations to short time, $\mathbf{u}^{\mathrm{j}} \approx 0$ , so that$$\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \eta^{z}}{\mathrm {d} t^{2}} = - \mathrm {R}_{0 z 0}^{z} \eta^{z}.$$The Riemann component for a weak field is easily calculated$$\mathsf {R}_{0 z 0}^{\mathsf {z}} = \frac {\partial^{2}}{\partial \mathsf {z}^{2}} \left(- \frac {\mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {z}}\right) = - \frac {2 \mathsf {M}}{\mathsf {z}^{3}}.$$Due to the E field, there is an acceleration of the lower particle$$\underline {\underline {\mathbf {a}}} = \frac {\mathbf {q}}{\mathbf {m}} \mathbf {E} \underline {\underline {\mathbf {e}}}_{z}.$$Thus$$\frac {\mathrm {d}^{2} \eta^{z}}{\mathrm {d t}^{2}} = \frac {2 \mathrm {M}}{z^{3}} \eta^{z} - \frac {\mathrm {q}}{\mathrm {m}} \mathrm {E}.$$It may seem disturbing at first that the two terms look as if they can be comparable. But suppose our laboratory is limited to a chunk of spacetime $\Delta \mathbf{x}^{\mu} \approx \mathbf{L}$ . Then at the end of the experiment the gravitational forces will have caused a change in $\eta^{z}$ :$$\delta \eta^{z} \sim \frac {1}{2} \frac {2 \mathbb {M}}{z^{3}} \eta^{z} \mathrm {L}^{2}.$$But $\eta^z < L$ so$$\delta \eta^{z} = \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {L}^{3}).$$The change in $\eta^z$ due to the electric field is$$\delta \eta^{z} \sim - \frac {\mathrm {q}}{\mathrm {m}} \cdot \mathrm {E} \cdot \frac {1}{2} \mathrm {L}^{2} = \mathcal {O} (\mathrm {L}^{2})$$so, if we sufficiently limit the “‘size’” of our experiment we can ignore gravitational effects.
